summary: Ronacher explains why he switched his main dev machine from Linux/macOS to a self-built Windows desktop, despite anticipating controversy. He argues that Windows can be made to feel UNIX-like with Cygwin and a few configuration tweaks for editors, version control, and SSH. He highlights performance and usability wins on Windows, especially for IDEs and desktop responsiveness, compared to Linux and OS X. The tradeoffs include weaker Windows support in Python libraries and social friction from open‑source peers. He concludes that the setup now works well for him and that the move will push him to improve Windows compatibility in his own projects.
key_points: Windows can be adapted to a UNIX-style workflow using Cygwin, PATH tweaks, and shell tooling.
Version control and SSH workflows on Windows require explicit configuration to match Linux conveniences.
Windows delivers noticeably better desktop and IDE performance than Linux/OS X in his experience.
The biggest costs are poorer Windows support in Python libraries and community stigma.
Switching platforms reshapes priorities, including a renewed focus on Windows compatibility.
money_quotes: Because I'm kinda annoyed by the bad state of the Linux desktop lately I decided to give Windows Vista a try.
A better choice is cygwin which installs a GNU user land with all the stuff you want on your system.
It's interesting how many things you're not missing until you rediscover them.
I don't know what the developers are doing that these two IDEs suck that much on non-Windows platforms, but the experience on a Windows system is great.
The worst is probably that a lot of the Python libraries I was using so far have a bad Windows support, including mine.
The other negative experience was that countless open-source zealots treat you like an outcast if you're working on Windows.
Anyhow. Works for me now and on the upside you can expect improved versions of the pocoo libraries regarding Windows-compatibility now :-)
key_insight: With a few UNIX-like tools and tweaks, Windows can be a fast, productive dev environment, but library support and community attitudes remain the real pain points.
